mnemonics.title: RSA Artists
mnemonics.content: R ivest S hamir A dleman - Remember as 'R iya S ings A nglo' to recall the three inventors of RSA algorithm

mnemonics.title: Public Locks, Private Unlocks
mnemonics.content: Public Key LOCKS the message (encrypts), Private Key UNLOCKS it (decrypts) - 'PUP' = Public Unlocks, Private Locks (actually reverse: Public Encrypts, Private Decrypts)

mnemonics.title: Diffie-Hellman Color Mix
mnemonics.content: D iffie- H ellman: Remember as 'D ark H orses' - two parties start with secret colors, exchange mixed colors, combine with their secret to create shared key

mnemonics.title: PKI Party
mnemonics.content: P KI = Public Key Infrastructure - Remember as 'P ublic K eys I nfrastructure' - the ecosystem managing public keys and certificates

mnemonics.title: Sign and Seal
mnemonics.content: Digital Signature: Sender 'S igs' with Private Key, Receiver 'V erifies' with Public Key - Remember: 'Sign Private, Verify Public'

acronyms.usage: Most widely used asymmetric algorithm - used for both encryption and digital signatures. Remember: First letters of inventors' surnames
Framework of roles, policies, hardware, software to manage keys and certificates. Used when you need trust in online communications
Key exchange protocol - allows two parties to establish shared secret over insecure channel. Not for encryption, just key exchange
Encryption program providing cryptographic privacy and authentication. Used for email security and file encryption

acronyms.expansion: Secure Sockets Layer / Transport Layer Security
acronyms.usage: Protocols for securing communications over networks. Uses asymmetric encryption for key exchange, symmetric for data transfer
Entity that issues digital certificates. Trust anchor in PKI - verifies that public key belongs to claimed entity
Message sent to CA to apply for digital certificate. Contains public key and entity information

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a KEYBOARD with two keys: one key has a LOCK icon (Public) - anyone can lock, the other has a KEY icon (Private) - only you can unlock. Visualize them as matching pair: golden key and silver lock

Picture a MAILBOX: Sender writes letter (plaintext), puts it in envelope, locks with recipient's PUBLIC key (mailbox slot is public), locks it. Only recipient with PRIVATE key (mailbox key) can open and read
Think of a WAX SEAL: Sender melts wax, presses signet ring (PRIVATE key), sends seal. Recipient checks if seal matches sender's design (PUBLIC key) - if matches, authentic

Imagine two people each have secret color paint. They send mixed color to each other. Each adds their secret color to received mix, resulting in SAME final color (shared secret) without either knowing other's original
Think of a KINGDOM: King (Root CA) gives authority to Lords (Intermediate CAs), who certify Knights (End entities). You trust Knight because you trust Lord, who was certified by King

analogies.analogy: A MAILBOX: The mailbox slot is the PUBLIC KEY - anyone can drop letters (encrypt) through it, but only the owner with the PRIVATE KEY (actual key) can open the mailbox to read the letters (decrypt)

A LOCKED BOX WITH PADLOCK: You want to send a secure package. You ask recipient for their open padlock (PUBLIC KEY), you lock the box with it, send it. Only recipient has the key (PRIVATE KEY) to open it
WAX SEALS ON ROYAL LETTERS: Ancient kings used personal seals - only they had the ring (PRIVATE KEY). Anyone could check if seal is genuine (PUBLIC KEY verification), proving letter truly from king

TWO BLIND FRIENDS WITH PAINT: Two friends want same color without meeting. Each picks secret color, mixes with common color, sends mix to other. Each adds secret color to received mix - both end up with identical combined color

analogies.concept: Certificate Authorities as Trusted Third Parties
analogies.analogy: GOVERNMENT ISSUING PASSPORTS: You trust a person because government (CA) issued their passport (certificate). Government verifies identity, stamps passport - you trust the document, therefore you trust the person
